title: practical english usage - Part 94
content:
4 misrelated        participles:       Looking        out    of   the    window, Knowing her pretty well, I realised something was wrong.

Not wishing to continue my studies, I decided to become a dress designer.

Being unable to help in any other way, I gave her some money.

participle clause usually expresses reason or cause.

which  are not normally used in progressive tenses (►  4). In these cases, the Note that -ing clauses can be made with verbs like be, have, wish and know, completely ruined our trip.) It rained for two weeks on end, completely ruining our trip. (= ... so that it down my  newspaper, ...) Putting down my newspaper, I walked over to the window. (= After I had put Having failed my medical exams, I took up teaching. (= As I had failed ...) Used economically, one can will last for six weeks. (= ɪf it is used ...) is needed to signal it.) Adverbial participle clauses are usually rather formal.

of course, when the idea of condition, reason, etc is so clear that no conjunction expressing condition, reason, time relations, result, etc. (This can only happen, Participle clauses can also be used in similar ways to full adverbial clauses, 3 adverbial       clauses:     Putting        down      my    paper,       I. ..

Do you know anybody who's lost a cat? (     D  you know anybody having Perfect participles are not often used in this way.

Half of the people invited to the party didn't turn up. (= .. . who  were Anyone touching that wire will get a shock. (= Anyone who  touches .. .) Who's the girl dancing with your brother? (= .. . the girl who  is dancing ...) Participle clauses used like this are ‘ reduced relative clauses' (►  237.11).

I found him  sitting at a table covered with papers.

In came the first runner, closely followed by the second.

There's Neville, eating as usual.

We can offer you a job cleaning cars.

Participle clauses can be used after nouns and pronouns.

2 after    nouns:    the    people        invited        to   the    party -ing                                              Section10 Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles:     Other    Uses grammar •  115  participle clauses For clauses like when ready, ►    251.5.

Leave in oven until cooked to a light brown colour.

Once deprived of oxygen, the brain dies.

When opened, consume within three days.

If asked to look after luggage for someone else, inform police at once.

while, once and until.

Clauses with past participles are possible (mostly in a formal style) after if, when, She struck me as being a very nervy kind of person.

They left without saying goodbye.

On  being introduced, British people often shake hands.

When telephoning from abroad, dial 1865, not 01865.

She's been quite different since coming back from America.

Switch off printer before replacing roller.

After having annoyed everybody he went home.

After talking to you I always feel better.

and as.

common with after, before, since, when, while, on, without, instead of, in spite of -ing clauses can be used after many conjunctions and prepositions. They  are 6 participle       clauses      after    conjunctions         and   prepositions the house seems pretty empty.

With Daniel working in Birmingham, and Lucy travelling most of the week, A car roared past with smoke pouring from the exhaust.

accompanying circumstances.

The  subject is often introduced by with when the clause expresses Hands held high, the dancers circle to the right.

A little girl walked past, her doll dragging behind her on the pavement.

All the money having been spent, we started looking for work.

Nobody having any more to say, the meeting was closed.

formal style.

A  participle clause can have its own subject. This happens most often in a rather 5  participle       clauses      with    their    own    subjects Taking everything into consideration, they ought to get another chance.

Supposing there was a war, what would you do?

Considering everything it wasn't a bad year.

Judging from his expression, he's in a bad mood.

Broadly speaking, dogs are more faithful than cats.
